does: This paper presents a circuit discovery pipeline that inserts Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) and Transcoders into neural networks to linearize their computation graphs, enabling precise causal analysis without linear approximation. By representing activations as sparse features, the approach transforms the typically non-linear OV (output-value) and MLP circuits into linear operations, allowing exact computation of each node's causal contribution. The method identifies both end-to-end circuits (full input-to-output paths) and local circuits (subgraph structures), and uses Hierarchical Attribution for scalable analysis. Applied to GPT-2 Small, it uncovers circuit mechanisms for bracket matching, induction, and indirect object identification tasks, revealing novel mechanistic details underlying previously discovered behaviors.
connects: This work builds on circuit analysis in mechanistic interpretability, a field focused on reverse-engineering neural network computations. It extends prior circuit discovery methods by addressing their reliance on linear approximations and gradient-based masking, which can miss important causal relationships. The paper leverages Sparse Autoencoders, developed in recent interpretability work to identify interpretable features in transformer activations, applying them in a novel way to achieve true linearity rather than approximation. It responds to limitations in prior end-to-end circuit work like IOI (Indirect Object Identification) and induction circuit studies by providing a more precise attribution framework. The use of hierarchical attribution connects to multi-scale analysis methods in interpretability, enabling analysis from local feature interactions to global model behavior.
enables: This linearization approach opens directions for more precise mechanistic interpretability at scale. By removing dependence on approximations, future work can confidently attribute model behaviors to specific computational subgraphs, potentially automating circuit discovery across diverse tasks and model architectures. The SAE-based framework could enable discovery of novel circuit structures not yet hypothesized by researchers. Applications include adversarial robustness analysis by identifying vulnerability-critical circuits, model editing through targeted circuit modification, and debugging unexpected model behaviors. The technique may scale to larger models, supporting interpretability of frontier language models. Hierarchical attribution could inform architecture design by revealing which computational patterns are essential, and the precise causal graphs could enable verification of safety properties in AI systems by confirming expected computational structures.
